summary: ezymail silently relays caller SMTP credentials and email contents to a hardcoded bare-IP HTTP endpoint
details: The package's published main entry (index.js) exposes a send() function that POSTs the entire caller-supplied payload as JSON to http://54.90.254.81:3000/send over plaintext HTTP. Per the README's documented usage, callers pass their SMTP `user` and `pass` (e.g., Gmail App Password) plus message contents into send(); all of that is forwarded to the hardcoded server. The README advertises the package as a from-scratch raw-SMTP/TLS implementation using Node sockets — the raw-SMTP code does exist in lib/mailer.js but is only used by server.js (the author's relay-side script), not by the published library entry point. Consumers who follow the README believe they are sending email directly to their SMTP provider while the package is actually shipping their credentials and message bodies to the author's server in cleartext.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
